1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
20	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
21	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
22	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
23	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
24	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
25	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
26	
27	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
28	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
29	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
30	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
31	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
32	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
33	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
34	\[
35	  \begin{array}{ccc}
36	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
37	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
38	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
39	  \end{array}
40	\]
41	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
42	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
43	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
44	
45	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
46	
47	\begin{definition}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
51	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
53	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_functor}
54	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
55	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
56	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
57	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
59	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
60	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
61	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
62	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
63	  % the unit ideal, see
64	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
65	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
66	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
67	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
68	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
69	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
70	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
71	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
72	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
73	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
74	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
75	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
76	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
77	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
78	  \[
79	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
80	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
81	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
82	  \]
83	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
84	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
85	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
86	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
87	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
88	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
89	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
90	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
91	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
92	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
93	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
94	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
95	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
96	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
97	\end{definition}
98	
99	\begin{definition}
100	
101	\leanok
102	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
103	  \label{def:cech_complex}
104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
105	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
106	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
107	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
108	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
110	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
111	  % is identified with the complex
112	  % $$
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
115	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
116	  % \ldots
117	  % $$
118	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
119	  % \begin{equation}
120	  % \label{equation-extended}
121	  % 0 \to
122	  % M \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
126	  % \ldots
127	  % \end{equation}
128	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
129	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
130	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
131	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
132	  vanishing).}
133	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
134	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
135	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
136	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
137	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
138	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
139	  in degree \(p\) it is
140	  \[
141	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
142	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
143	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
144	  \]
145	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
146	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
147	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
148	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
149	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
150	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
151	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
152	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
153	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
154	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
155	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
156	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
157	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
158	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
159	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
160	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
161	\end{definition}
162	
163	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
164	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
165	
166	It is convenient to factor the construction of
167	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
168	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the entire mathematical
169	content that is not yet available off the shelf is isolated in the middle piece.
170	
171	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
172	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
173	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
174	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
175	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
176	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
177	\[
178	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
179	    \;=\;
180	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
181	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
182	\]
183	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
184	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
185	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
186	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
187	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
188	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
189	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
190	
191	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
192	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
193	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
194	\[
195	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
196	  \qquad
197	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
198	\]
199	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
200	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
201	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
202	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
203	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
204	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
205	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
206	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
207	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
208	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
209	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
210	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
211	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
212	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
213	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
214	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. This
215	functoriality is \emph{not} coupled to the project's tensor--pullback substrate;
216	it consumes only Mathlib's pseudofunctor packaging of pullback and pushforward.
217	Concretely, pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
218	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
219	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
220	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
222	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts about this
223	packaging: the mate identity expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback
224	comparison as the pushforward comparison (the lemma
225	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}), its identity normalisation
226	\texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}, and the pseudofunctor unitor and pentagon
227	(associativity) coherences \texttt{pseudofunctor\_left\_unitality},
228	\texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality}, \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}.
229	All of these are already available off the shelf, so the functoriality of the
230	relative-direct-image functor is a \emph{consumer of Mathlib's coherence},
231	independent of the project-local sheafification/pullback machinery of the
232	tensor--pullback substrate. The laws are nonetheless not formal trivialities: each
233	is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison isomorphisms along
234	the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and past the adjunction
235	unit, gluing them across two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions; the obstacle is the
236	bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pushforward/pullback pseudofunctor coherence supplied by
254	Mathlib (the comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities), not any project-local ingredient.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}\leanok
277	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
278	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
280	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
281	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
282	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
283	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
284	  \[
285	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
286	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
287	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
288	  \]
289	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
290	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
291	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
292	\end{definition}
293	
294	\begin{definition}\leanok
295	[Push--pull object map]
296	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
298	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
299	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
300	  \[
301	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
302	  \]
303	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{definition}\leanok
307	[Push--pull comparison map]
308	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
310	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
311	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
312	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
313	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
314	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
315	  \[
316	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
318	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
320	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \;=\;
322	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
323	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
324	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
325	  \]
326	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
327	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
328	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
329	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
330	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
331	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}\leanok
335	[Push--pull functor assembly]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_functor}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
339	  % NOTE (iter-264): only `pushPullMap_id` exists in Lean (landed axiom-clean this iter);
340	  % `pushPullMap_comp` (the pentagon law) is still deferred to an in-file comment, NOT a
341	  % declaration. The statement-block \leanok therefore over-states this lemma. Plan agent:
342	  % either split this block into two (one \lean{} pin each) so \leanok tracks them
343	  % independently, or have a `lemma pushPullMap_comp ... := sorry` stub added to the Lean file.
344	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `def:cech_nerve` from this \uses. The
345	  % functor laws `pushPullMap_id`/`pushPullMap_comp` (L216/278 of the Lean file)
346	  % depend only on push_pull_obj/push_pull_map and the mate lemmas; their
347	  % signatures never mention `CechNerve`. The dependency runs the OTHER way:
348	  % `CechNerve` is BUILT FROM the push-pull functor (def:cech_nerve \uses
349	  % lem:push_pull_functor), so listing def:cech_nerve here created a
350	  % cech_nerve <-> push_pull_functor cycle that crashed `leanblueprint web`.
351	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
352	  The object and morphism assignments of
353	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} assemble into a
354	  functor \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\): the
355	  comparison maps respect identities and composition,
356	  \[
357	    G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)},
358	    \qquad
359	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g),
360	  \]
361	  the latter for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of
362	  \(X\)-schemes. Composing \(G\) with the backbone of
363	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
364	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
365	\end{lemma}
366	
367	\begin{proof}
368	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
369	  Both laws are statements purely about the pushforward/pullback comparison
370	  isomorphisms and carry no sectionwise content. The route is the adjunction-mate
371	  calculus applied to the head \(p_{1*}(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward
372	  comparison in the composite defining \(G(g)\): rewriting this head as the
373	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison
374	  \((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) — via \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv} —
375	  turns both laws into identities among the pseudofunctor's structure \(2\)-cells.
376	
377	  For \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}\): after the mate rewrite the identity leg
378	  collapses by the identity normalisation \texttt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackId\_hom}
379	  together with the right unitality \texttt{pseudofunctor\_right\_unitality};
380	  the two over-triangle coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
381	
382	  For \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\): the same mate rewrite reduces the claim to
383	  reassociating the three pullback comparisons, which is the pseudofunctor pentagon
384	  \texttt{pseudofunctor\_associativity}; the inner adjunction unit is slid past the
385	  outer pushforward by naturality of the unit (\(\mathrm{Adjunction.unit\_naturality}\)).
386	
387	  The content is entirely Mathlib-side pseudofunctor coherence; the work is the
388	  bookkeeping of the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle
389	  \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\). No step invokes the project-local sheafification/pullback
390	  square, so the laws are independent of the tensor--pullback substrate.
391	\end{proof}

393	\begin{lemma}
394	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
395	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
396	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
397	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
398	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
400	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
401	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
402	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
403	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
404	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
405	  \[
406	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
407	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
408	    \;=\;
409	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
410	  \]
411	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
412	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
413	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
414	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
415	\end{lemma}
416	
417	\begin{proof}
418	  Proved directly in Lean.
419	\end{proof}

421	\begin{lemma}
422	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
423	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
425	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
426	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
427	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
429	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
430	  collapses to the transport-light form
431	  \[
432	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_2
434	    \;=\;
435	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
436	      \;\circ\; e_3,
437	  \]
438	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
439	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
440	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
441	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
442	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
443	  composition (pentagon) law.
444	\end{lemma}
445	
446	\begin{proof}
447	  Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}

450	\begin{definition}\leanok
451	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
452	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
453	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
454	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
455	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
456	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
457	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
458	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
459	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
460	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
461	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
462	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
463	\end{definition}

465	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
466	
467	\begin{lemma}
468	
469	\leanok
470	[{\v C}ech acyclicity on affines]
471	  \label{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechAcyclic.affine}
473	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
474	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG (affine vanishing);
475	  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover
476	  % {\v C}ech vanishing) and Lemma
477	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (Serre vanishing on affines)
478	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L44--52 and L145--155).
479	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
480	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
481	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
482	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.
483	  % Then $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for
484	  % all $p > 0$."
485	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Serre vanishing: Higher cohomology vanishes on affine schemes
486	  % for quasi-coherent modules.
487	  % Let $X$ be a scheme.
488	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
489	  % For any affine open $U \subset X$ we have
490	  % $H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for all $p > 0$."
491	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
492	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
493	  vanishing) and Stacks, Tag 02KG
494	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, Serre vanishing on
495	  affines).}
496	  Let \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
497	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module, and
498	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) a standard open cover (so the
499	  \(f_i \in A\) generate the unit ideal). Then the {\v C}ech complex
500	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) has vanishing cohomology
501	  in all positive degrees:
502	  \[
503	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
504	  \]
505	  and consequently \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\).
506	\end{lemma}
507	
508	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
509	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
510	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
511	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
512	% ...
513	% It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
514	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
515	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
516	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
517	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
518	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
519	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
520	% Let us define a homotopy
521	% $$
522	% h :
523	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
524	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}
525	% \longrightarrow
526	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
527	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_p}, \mathfrak p}
528	% $$
529	% by the rule
530	% $$
531	% h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}
532	% $$
533	% ... We compute ... $(dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ ...
534	% This proves the identity map is homotopic to zero as desired."
535	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We are going to apply
536	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}.
537	% As our basis $\mathcal{B}$ for the topology of $X$ we are going to use
538	% the affine opens of $X$. ... Note that the intersection of standard opens
539	% in an affine is another standard open. Hence property (1) holds. ...
540	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
541	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
542	\begin{proof}
543	  \uses{def:cech_complex}
544	  Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) for an
545	  \(A\)-module \(M\). By Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} the {\v C}ech complex of
546	  the standard cover is the complex of localisations
547	  \[
548	    \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
549	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\;
550	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1} f_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
551	  \]
552	  and the claim \(\check{H}^p = 0\) for \(p > 0\) is equivalent to exactness of the
553	  extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \cdots\). Exactness may
554	  be checked after localising at an arbitrary prime \(\mathfrak{p} \subset A\).
555	  Choose an index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) with
556	  \(f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\); then
557	  \(M_{f_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}, \mathfrak{p}} = M_{\mathfrak{p}}\), and the
558	  prescription \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\)
559	  defines a contracting homotopy of the localised extended complex: a direct
560	  computation gives \((dh + hd)(s) = s\), so the identity is homotopic to zero and
561	  the localised complex is exact. As exactness holds after every localisation, the
562	  extended complex is exact and \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for
563	  \(p > 0\).
564	
565	  For the sheaf-cohomology statement, take the affine opens of \(U\) as a basis of
566	  the topology and the standard open covers as the admissible covers. Intersections
567	  of standard opens are again standard opens, standard covers are cofinal among
568	  covers of a basic open, and the {\v C}ech vanishing just proved supplies the
569	  acyclicity hypothesis; the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis then
570	  yields \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\). \emph{(This is Serre
571	  vanishing for quasi-coherent sheaves on affine schemes.)}
572	
573	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
574	  infrastructure: the explicit description of the standard-cover {\v C}ech complex
575	  as the complex of localisations \(\prod M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}}
576	  \to \cdots\) together with the prime-local, module-level contracting homotopy
577	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\) witnessing its
578	  positive-degree exactness, neither of which is available for sheaves of modules
579	  on a scheme.}
580	\end{proof}

582	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
583	
584	\begin{lemma}
585	
586	\leanok
587	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
588	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
589	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
590	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
591	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
592	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
593	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
594	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
595	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
596	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
597	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
598	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
599	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
600	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
601	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
602	  % $$
603	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
604	  % $$
605	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
606	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
607	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
608	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
609	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
610	  % we have
611	  % $$
612	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
613	  % $$
614	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
615	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
616	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
617	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
618	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
619	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
620	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
621	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
622	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
623	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
624	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
625	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
626	  \[
627	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
628	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
629	  \]
630	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
631	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
632	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
633	
634	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
635	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
636	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
637	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
638	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
639	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
640	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
641	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
642	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
643	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
644	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
645	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
646	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
647	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
648	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
649	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
650	  role in the applications.
651	\end{lemma}
652	
653	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In view of
654	% Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
655	% this is a special case of
656	% Cohomology, Lemma
657	% \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence-application}."
658	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Consider the Leray spectral sequence
659	% $E_2^{p, q} = H^p(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})$
660	% converging to $H^{p + q}(X, \mathcal{F})$, see
661	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-Leray}.
662	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}
663	% we see that the sheaves $R^qf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent.
664	% By Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}
665	% we see that $E_2^{p, q} = 0$ when $p > 0$.
666	% Hence the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$ and we win."
667	\begin{proof}
668	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, def:cech_complex}
669	  The question is local on \(S\), and the formation of both sides commutes with
670	  restriction to opens of \(S\), so we may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is
671	  affine. Because \(f\) is separated and quasi-compact, each finite intersection
672	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(f\) restricted to it is an affine
673	  morphism; by the affine acyclicity of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} the
674	  higher direct images of \(\mathcal{F}\) over each such intersection vanish, so the
675	  terms of the relative {\v C}ech complex carry no hidden higher cohomology.
676	
677	  Consider the {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence of the cover
678	  \(\mathfrak{U}\), with \(E_2\)-page
679	  \(E_2^{p,q} = \check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\)
680	  converging to \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\). Since all intersections are affine,
681	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} forces the presheaf cohomology
682	  \(\underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})\) to vanish on the intersections for \(q > 0\), so
683	  the spectral sequence collapses to its row \(q = 0\): the {\v C}ech cohomology of
684	  \(\mathcal{F}\) for the cover \(\mathfrak{U}\) computes the sheaf cohomology,
685	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\),
686	  and the same identification holds relatively, i.e.\ the cohomology sheaves of
687	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) compute the cohomology
688	  sheaves of \(Rf_*\mathcal{F}\).
689	
690	  It remains to identify these cohomology sheaves with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\). Over
691	  the affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the Leray spectral sequence
692	  \(E_2^{p,q} = H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) \Rightarrow H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\)
693	  has \(R^q f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent, whence \(H^p(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F}) = 0\)
694	  for \(p > 0\) by Serre vanishing (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}). The Leray
695	  sequence therefore degenerates at \(E_2\), giving
696	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\). Combining with the previous
697	  paragraph, \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
698	  \cong R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules for all \(i \geq 0\).
699	
700	  \emph{This proof depends on the following currently-absent Mathlib
701	  infrastructure: the {\v C}ech-to-derived-functor spectral sequence and the Leray
702	  spectral sequence for the category of sheaves of modules on a scheme, neither of
703	  which is presently available there; the argument uses both to collapse onto the
704	  comparison isomorphism.}
705	\end{proof}
706

=== HigherDirectImage.lean ===
/-
Copyright (c) 2026 Christian Merten. All rights reserved.
Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Christian Merten
-/
import Mathlib

/-!
# Higher direct images `Rⁱ f_*` of quasi-coherent sheaves (`i ≥ 1`)

This file treats the higher derived direct images `Rⁱ f_* F` for `i ≥ 1`,
complementing the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of `Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean`.

Throughout, `f : X ⟶ S` is a morphism of schemes and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of
modules on `X`. The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` is the `i`-th right
derived functor of the pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.

This file supplies the single declaration needed downstream by the Čech
computation of higher direct images:

* `AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage` — the higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F`,
  the derived-functor object that `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` compares the
  Čech complex against.

See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex`.

Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Definition of `Rⁱ f_*`.
-/

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

open Scheme.Modules

variable {S X : Scheme.{u}}

/-- The `i`-th higher direct image `Rⁱ f_* F` of a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`
along a morphism `f : X ⟶ S`, defined as the `i`-th right derived functor of the
pushforward functor `f_*` applied to `F`.

For `i = 0` this recovers the ordinary pushforward `R⁰ f_* F = f_* F`.

Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Definition of `Rⁱ f_*`. -/
noncomputable def higherDirectImage [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
    (f : X ⟶ S) (i : ℕ) (F : X.Modules) : S.Modules :=
  ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F

end AlgebraicGeometry

5	read_only: true

7	default_enabled: false

9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase, and every dag phase that
11	    touched STRATEGY.md. When you do dispatch me, do so AFTER you've
12	    finished writing / confirming STRATEGY.md and BEFORE any writer /
13	    refactor / prover dispatch this iteration. My verdict is what you act

20	      - STRATEGY.md is unchanged since the prior iter's verbatim
21	        content (SHA-equal — not just "no new substantive edits");
22	      - my prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE or REJECT;

28	    with a one-liner naming the conditions met, e.g.:

30	    prior verdict was SOUND with no live CHALLENGE``. Filling templates
31	    with hollow dispatches when nothing has changed is exactly the

33	  - **Strict context discipline.** My value comes from a fresh view of
34	    the strategy. Your directive must contain ONLY:

43	    - `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, recent prover task results.
44	    - Recent review reports or session journals.

48	    cost is exactly what I'm meant to challenge.

50	    route, you must either (a) update STRATEGY.md to address the
51	    challenge, or (b) record an explicit rebuttal in
52	    `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my challenge does not apply.
53	    Skipping the rebuttal step is the planner's failure.

61	
62	# Strategy Critic
63	
64	You are the fresh-context strategy critic. You read the project's `STRATEGY.md` with **no exposure to its iter-by-iter history** and challenge the strategic choices as if a mathematician encountering this project for the first time would.
65	
66	Your job is to be the project's adversarial reader. The plan agent has been in the project's context for many iterations and is naturally invested in the existing routes. You are the corrective.
67	
68	## Your context discipline
69	
70	Your directive will name ONLY:
71	
72	- The current `STRATEGY.md` (the primary subject of your review).
73	- The project's stated final goal (one paragraph naming the theorem(s) to formalize).
74	- A short reference index (`references/summary.md` content).
75	- A blueprint summary (chapter titles + one-line topic per chapter).
76	
77	Your directive will NOT include `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, prover task results, review summaries, or recent narrative. **If the directive accidentally includes any of these, ignore them.** Your value depends entirely on the lack of pollution. Reading the iter-by-iter history would make you the planner; the planner already exists. Stay fresh.
78	
79	You may use:
80	
81	- The references named in the directive (read them if needed for the math).
82	- The blueprint chapter summaries (the prose summary in the directive, NOT the full chapter text — full-chapter audit is the territory of the blueprint-review subagent in the catalog).
83	- `archon-lean-lsp` if you need to spot-check a Mathlib name's existence.
84	
85	You may NOT use:
86	
87	- Iter sidecars, even if you find their paths.
88	- Any state file other than what the directive named.
89	
90	## What you check
91	
92	For each strategic route in `STRATEGY.md`:
93	
94	1. **Goal-alignment.** Does this route, if executed, actually produce the project's stated final goal? Or is there a subtle gap where the route's end-state isn't quite what the goal demands?
95	
96	2. **Mathematical soundness.** Is the route's mathematical argument plausible? Are there steps that look like they assume something the route's prerequisites don't give you? A fresh reader is good at spotting "wait, this step needs X but X isn't established."
97	
98	3. **Alternative routes.** Are there other approaches the strategy doesn't mention? For each, briefly note: what it would look like, why it might be cheaper or sounder, and why the strategy may have rejected it (if you can tell from prose).
99	
100	4. **Sunk-cost reasoning.** Does the strategy justify a route in terms of "we've already done X" rather than "X is the right way"? That's a sunk-cost smell. Call it out by name.
101	
102	5. **Prerequisite assumptions.** Does the strategy assume Mathlib infrastructure that may not exist? Verify the named lemmas / type classes / structures actually exist (use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for spot-checks). Strategy that depends on phantom Mathlib infra is invalid.
103	
104	6. **Effort estimates.** If the strategy carries per-route LOC or iteration estimates, do they look honest given the scope of the route? Estimates that are wildly under-counted (e.g. "200 LOC for representability of Pic") indicate either underestimated effort or a misplanned route. The LOC cell is a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`); flag rows whose LOC range is grossly inconsistent with `Iters left` given the route's scope — e.g. a sprawling representability phase marked `~30` LOC with `Iters left: 1`. Cross-check the realized LOC of comparable rows in `## Completed` (when present) against the active estimates: a remaining phase of similar scope to a completed one but estimated at a fraction of its realized LOC is a dishonest-estimate signal.
105	
106	7. **Infrastructure-deferral patterns.** This is a distinct failure mode from sunk-cost and must be checked independently. An infrastructure-deferral pattern is present when any of the following hold:
107	
108	   - A route pivot changes the surface approach but the hardest prerequisite (a missing Mathlib construction, an unproven foundational lemma, a typeclass that doesn't exist) is the same before and after the pivot. The pivot renames the problem without solving it. To check: identify the hardest prerequisite in the current route and the hardest prerequisite in the route it replaced. If they are the same construction or the same gap, the pivot is avoidance.
109	
110	   - A required construction is described as "off-critical path", "future work", "deferred pending upstream Mathlib PR", "exceptional case", or similar language, while the project's stated goal provably requires it. The project's goal is not negotiable; if a construction is necessary for the final theorem, it is on the critical path by definition, regardless of what the strategy claims. Check by asking: does the final theorem statement in `## Goal` hold without the deferred construction? If no, the deferral is a goal weakening dressed as a strategic decision.
111	
112	   - The strategy splits the goal into a "main case" and one or more "exceptional cases", proves or plans to prove the main case, and defers the exceptional cases indefinitely. Unless the exceptional cases are genuinely independent theorems not required by `## Goal`, this is a goal weakening. Check the statement in `## Goal` — if it asserts something for ALL cases, each case is required.
113	
114	   - The strategy describes building infrastructure for a weaker or different statement than `## Goal` requires, with the intention of extending later. Plans requiring later extension of core infrastructure almost never complete the extension — flag as CHALLENGE.
115	
116	   - The strategy proposes huge phases and is reluctant to start working on them, without decomposing them into concrete sub-phases that it could have started on. This is a deferral pattern because it will always assume this phase it too difficult, but in pratice it should think like a mathlib contributor and decompose the phase into intermediate phases.
117	
118	   - A phase row in `## Phases & estimations` has `Iters left: ?` (or an estimate-free / never-shrinking cell) AND has been in that state without any active prover lane and without any concrete progress in the blueprints. Stagnant phases are infrastructure-deferral by inaction.
119	
120	   For each infrastructure-deferral finding: name the specific construction being deferred, confirm whether the stated goal requires it, and state whether any route in the strategy actually builds it with a concrete timeline. A construction deferred to "Mathlib upstream" with no project-side plan and no timeline is an unresolved gap in the project's strategy, not an accepted dependency.
121	
122	8. **Parallelism under-exploitation.** If the project has multiple independent files or proof obligations that are not sequentially dependent, but the strategy routes them through a single sequential phase, flag this as a throughput risk. Parallelism in prover dispatch is a planning obligation, not an optimization. A strategy that serializes independent work is implicitly estimating 2–3× the needed iter count.
123	
124	9. **Format compliance.** `STRATEGY.md` must follow the canonical skeleton documented in the plan prompt. Violations to flag:
125	
126	   - **Size**: the file exceeds ~250 lines or ~12 KB.
127	   - **Headings**: the section list isn't exactly `## Goal`, `## Phases & estimations`, `## Completed`, `## Routes`, `## Open strategic questions`, `## Mathlib gaps & new material` (in that order). `## Completed` is optional — omitted while nothing is done yet — but when present it MUST sit between `## Phases & estimations` and `## Routes`. Renamed or other extra top-level sections (`## Project goal`, `## End-state`, `## Decomposition`, `## Roadmap`, `## Soundness rules`, etc.) are violations.
128	   - **Per-iter narrative**: references to specific iterations ("this iter we tried X", "last iter", "the iter-XYZ pivot") in prose. Per-iter history belongs in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, never in STRATEGY.md. (Bare iter numbers in the `## Completed` table's `Iters` cell — e.g. `294 · 8` — are fine; that is the ledger, not narrative.)
129	   - **Accumulation discipline**: a completed phase still sitting in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (it must MOVE to `## Completed`), an excised route still occupying a `## Routes` subsection, or freeform prose-history anywhere. The concise `## Completed` table is allowed and expected; what's flagged is completed work left in the active table, prose bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has ballooned past ~12 rows or grown multi-line cells.
130	   - **Table discipline**: `## Phases & estimations` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks, one short line per cell. `Status` must be a short inline tag (`ACTIVE`, `BLOCKED`, `PAUSED BY USER`, …), not prose; `LOC` must be a rough remaining-LOC range (e.g. `~80–220`), not a velocity figure. When present, `## Completed` must be a Markdown table with columns Phase | Iters (done@ · used) | LOC | Files | Key results | Reusable techniques | Pitfalls, again one short line per cell. Long prose in cells, or replacing either table with prose subsections, is a violation.
131	   - **Appendix sections**: "Historical decisions", "Considered alternatives", "Past iterations summary", "Lessons learned", or any other history-tracking section. Iter sidecars are where rejected alternatives live.
132	
133	   Format violations are reported under a synthetic "format" route — see the Report Format section below. **Format is not cosmetic.** A STRATEGY.md that drifts from the canonical skeleton bleeds into the plan agent's context every iter and makes the strategy itself harder to reason about. Treat material format violations as a CHALLENGE that must be resolved this iter via an in-place restructure (using iter sidecars to hold any historical detail that currently lives inline).
134	
135	## Directive Format
136	
137	```markdown

139	
140	## Slug
141	<slug>
142	
143	## Project goal
144	<one paragraph: the final theorem(s) or claim(s) the project is trying to formalize. Include the protected declarations if any.>

153

155	
156	<for each blueprint chapter, one line: filename + topic. NOT the chapter content — just the index.>
157	
158	## Prior critique status
159	
160	<one line per still-live prior-iter challenge. Format STRICTLY:
161	
162	  - <prior-iter-NNN>: <short challenge phrase> — live | addressed
163	
164	NO paraphrased planner responses, NO multi-paragraph re-litigation,
165	NO "the iter-XXX plan-agent considered pulling this to iter-YYY but
166	deferred to iter-ZZZ" — those are iter-by-iter narrative and would
167	contaminate the fresh-context audit. The point of "Prior critique
168	status" is solely to tell you which challenges to re-check, NOT how
169	the planner reasoned about them. If a prior critique is fully resolved
170	in the current STRATEGY.md, mark it "addressed"; if you disagree
171	after auditing, your report will flip it back to live.
172	
173	If first-iter (no prior critique), write "no prior critique".>
174	```
175	
176	**If you find this section contains planner-side reasoning, iter
177	narrative, or paraphrased plan-agent responses beyond the one-line
178	"live | addressed" tags, ignore that extra material.** Your fresh
179	context is your value; do not let leaked planner state colonize
180	your audit.
181	
182	## Report format
183	
184	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/strategy-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
185	
186	**Omit-empty rule.** Every section below is optional except `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict`. If a section's right answer is "nothing to report", **OMIT the section entirely** — do NOT write "none", "N/A", "no findings detected", or "(omit if empty)" as filler content. The absence of a section IS the signal that nothing was found there. Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is SOUND with no flagged items, render the block as just the verdict line and omit the bullet checklist above it.
187	
188	```markdown
189	# Strategy Critic Report
190	
191	## Slug
192	<slug>
193	
194	## Iteration
195	<NNN>
196	
197	## Routes audited
198	
199	For each strategic route in STRATEGY.md, one block:
200	
201	### Route: <name>
202	
203	- **Goal-alignment**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
204	- **Mathematical soundness**: PASS | PARTIAL | FAIL — <one line>
205	- **Sunk-cost reasoning detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name the sunk-cost claim verbatim>
206	- **Infrastructure-deferral detected**: yes | no — <if yes: name the deferred construction; state whether the goal requires it; state whether any route in the strategy builds it with a concrete timeline>
207	- **Phantom prerequisites**: <list any Mathlib infra the strategy assumes exists that you couldn't verify>

209	- **Parallelism under-exploited**: yes | no — <if yes, name the independent obligations being serialized>
210	- **Verdict**: SOUND | CHALLENGE | REJECT
211	  - SOUND: the route makes sense and the planner should proceed.
212	  - CHALLENGE: the route has issues the planner must address (in STRATEGY.md or via an explicit rebuttal in plan.md) before this iter ends.
213	  - REJECT: the route is fundamentally broken (goal-misaligned, mathematically unsound, or built on phantom prerequisites). Do not proceed on this route until the strategy is rewritten.

222	- **Accumulation detected**: yes | no — <if yes, name completed phases still in the active `## Phases & estimations` table (should be in `## Completed`), excised routes still present, prose-history bloat, or a `## Completed` table that has outgrown its bound>.
223	- **Table discipline**: PASS | FAIL — <if FAIL, describe the deviation>.
224	- **Appendix sections**: <list any detected; omit field if none>.
225	- **Format verdict**: COMPLIANT | DRIFTED | NON-COMPLIANT
226

245	- **Severity of the omission**: critical | major | minor

254

256	
257	Every CHALLENGE, every REJECT, every NON-COMPLIANT format verdict, and every infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE/REJECT lands here automatically. No under-classification.
258	
259	- Route <name>: CHALLENGE — <what the planner must address>.
260	- Route <name>: infrastructure-deferral CHALLENGE — <construction> required by goal, no concrete timeline. Planner must either build it this iter or produce a concrete plan with an iter estimate.
261	- Alternative <name>: critical omission — <one line>.
262	- Phantom prerequisite <name>: <one line>.
263	- Format: NON-COMPLIANT — <two or three most impactful deviations>. Restructure STRATEGY.md in-place this iter.
264	
265	## Overall verdict
266	
267	One paragraph. If infrastructure-deferral findings exist, name them explicitly — "the strategy defers X, which is required for the stated goal" must appear verbatim so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
268	```
269	
270	## Return value
271	
272	Your final assistant message:
273	
274	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHALLENGE/REJECT verdicts, <K> infrastructure-deferral findings, format=<COMPLIANT|DRIFTED|NON-COMPLIANT>`
275	- The path to your full report.
276	
277	## Reminders
278	
279	- **You are the project's adversarial reader.** Don't be polite. If a route is avoidance dressed as strategy, say so.
280	- **Infrastructure deferral is not strategy.** A construction required by the goal is on the critical path regardless of what the strategy labels it.
281	- **A pivot that moves the same hard problem one layer deeper is not a pivot.** Check the hardest prerequisite in the new route against the hardest prerequisite in the old route. If they match, flag it.
282	- **"Off-critical path" is a red flag, not a planning decision**, when the goal requires the deferred item.
283	- **Don't request more context.** Iter history is what you're meant to be free of.
284	- **Cite Mathlib precisely.** Use the LSP tools to verify before claiming a prerequisite exists or is missing.
285	- **Strict severity.** CHALLENGE and REJECT are must-fix; do not under-classify to keep momentum.
286	- **Be specific about alternatives.** "Maybe consider another approach" is useless. Name it.

13	# Strategy Auditor Subagent
14	
15	Your job is to prevent fundamental planning errors before prover budget is wasted. You act as a deep-reasoning gatekeeper, comparing the project's strategy against its mathematical references.
16	
17	## Your Tasks
18	
19	1. **Read the Actual Sources:**
20	   - You must read the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` files located in the `references/` directory.
21	   - The `.md` files in `references/` are merely pointers and summaries; use them to find the relevant paragraphs, but base your audit on the raw source material.
22	2. **Audit the Strategy:**
23	   - Read `STRATEGY.md` and the planner's directive.
24	   - Compare the proposed routes, case splits, and intermediate lemmas against the original source's structure.
25	3. **Flag Strategic Errors:**
26	   - **Unnecessary Case Splits:** Does the strategy split into cases (e.g., genus 0 vs >=1) when the reference handles it uniformly?
27	   - **Silent Assumptions:** Does the reference require a restrictive assumption (e.g., "we restrict to genus >= 2 because...") that the strategy silently ignores?
28	   - **Missing Prerequisites:** Does the reference introduce definitions or lemmas before the target theorem that the strategy failed to include?
29	   - **Hallucinated Routes:** Has the strategy invented a path that the book explicitly avoids or doesn't mention?
30	
31	Report all findings clearly so the planner can correct `STRATEGY.md` and its objectives. Return your outcome and the path to your report.
32
Let me read the blueprint-reviewer and mathlib-analogist descriptors to get their directive formats.

52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.

79	       writer was asked to fix.

81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)

106	    in the blueprint.

108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
